title: Boudoir vs. Glamour Photography: What's the Actual Difference?
h1: Boudoir vs. Glamour: What's the Actual Difference?
subtitle: Wardrobe, mood, intent, and the way each style is photographed. Plus — how to know which one you actually want, and why most sessions end up being a mix of both.
metaDescription: Boudoir and glamour photography aren't the same thing. Boudoir is intimate and unguarded; glamour is polished and finished. Here's how the wardrobe, mood, and intent differ — and why most clients want a mix of both.
cardTitle: Boudoir vs. Glamour Photography: What's the Actual Difference?
cardExcerpt: Boudoir is intimate and unguarded. Glamour is polished and finished. Here's how the wardrobe, mood, and intent differ — and why most sessions end up being a mix of both.
heroAlt: Bronze halter bodysuit, gold jewelry, velvet chaise — luxury glamour boudoir hybrid portrait
heroCaption: Where glamour and boudoir meet — and where most of my work actually lives
directAnswer: Boudoir is intimate, unguarded, and typically lingerie or less. Glamour is polished, finished, and typically clothed. Boudoir captures the moment before or after; glamour captures the moment of. Most of my clients book "boudoir" but want a glamour edge — old Hollywood lighting, statement jewelry, gowns and lingerie mixed in the same session. The line is real, but most great sessions cross it.
closerBody: The best part of working with a studio that shoots both is you don't have to pick a lane. We'll build a session that mixes the polished and the intimate — and you get to decide which images go where.

sections.h2: What Is the Difference Between Boudoir and Glamour Photography?
sections.answer: Wardrobe is the biggest tell. Boudoir is photographed in lingerie or less. Glamour is photographed in gowns, blazers, statement outfits. Mood is the second tell. Boudoir is unguarded and intimate — the room where a woman gets dressed. Glamour is the finished version — already dressed, already ready to go. One is the getting-ready. The other is the going-out.
prose: Both styles can be photographed with strength, beauty, and power. The difference is mostly in the energy you're trying to capture. Boudoir leans into something private and personal — the slow burn before an event, or the wind-down after. Glamour leans into the public-facing version of you — the version that walks into the room and is already seen.
The lighting can be the same. The poses can be the same. The client can be the same. What changes is what they're wearing and what state they're in.

sections.h2: Do You Shoot Both Boudoir and Glamour in One Session?
sections.answer: Yes — most of my sessions are a mix of both. The standard format is three outfits, three sets, with one leaning more glamour, one leaning more boudoir, and one somewhere in between. This gives clients images they can post publicly (the glamour ones) and images that stay more private (the boudoir ones).
prose: Most clients walk in thinking they want "boudoir" but discover during the consultation that what they actually want is the mix. They want one set in a gown they can hang on the wall. One set in lingerie that's just for them. One set somewhere in between — maybe an oversized button-down, jeans, a fur coat over a bra.
The advantage of mixing in one session is variety. You don't pay for three separate shoots. You get a full range of looks — from social-share-ready to private-keepsake — in the same day.

sections.h2: How Do You Help a Client Decide Between Boudoir and Glamour?
sections.answer: We talk about vibe, not technique. The question I ask is: do you want sexy and edgy, or cute and flirty, or soft and romantic? The answer points to the mix. Clients who say "I want to look powerful and beautiful in a dress" are usually after glamour. Clients who say "I want something just for me that I'd never share" are after boudoir. Most clients want both.
prose: There's no right answer to which style a client should book. There's only the right answer for them. I ask about the vibe, the comfort level with showing skin, what they want to do with the images afterward, and whether they have a partner they're shooting for or this is fully for themselves.

If a client says she's not ready for lingerie, we can still get the boudoir feel with an oversized button-down, jeans unbuttoned, a robe, a fur coat. The boudoir category is more about the mood than the amount of skin. We adjust accordingly.

sections.h2: Which Is More Popular at Your Studio — Boudoir or Glamour?
sections.answer: Boudoir is what clients book. But if you look at my actual work, most of it leans glamour. My style is old Hollywood — bold lighting, statement jewelry, glam hair and makeup, painted backdrops, fur coats and gowns. Even my boudoir shots are glamorous. The result: clients book boudoir, but they walk out with images that look like a magazine editorial. That's intentional.
prose: I want these images to last a lifetime. Trendy, hyper-sexy boudoir photographs the moment well but ages awkwardly. Old Hollywood glamour ages beautifully. Twenty years from now, I want my clients to look at these images and think damn, look how good Grandma looked, not cringe.
If a client specifically wants something edgier or more risqué, we absolutely can. The session is built around them. But the house style leans timeless on purpose.

faqs.q: What's the difference between boudoir and glamour photography?
faqs.a: Boudoir is intimate and unguarded, typically photographed in lingerie or less, capturing the moment before or after an event. Glamour is polished and finished, typically photographed in gowns or blazers, capturing the moment of.

faqs.q: Can I do both boudoir and glamour in one session?
faqs.a: Yes. Most of my sessions include three outfit changes — usually with one set leaning glamour, one leaning boudoir, and one in between. This gives clients a variety of images for different uses.

faqs.q: How do I know whether I want boudoir or glamour photos?
faqs.a: Start with the vibe: do you want sexy and edgy, cute and flirty, or soft and romantic? Then think about how you'd use the photos. If you want images for the wall and social media, glamour. If you want something private, boudoir. Most clients want a mix.
No. The boudoir feel is about mood and intimacy, not the amount of skin. You can shoot boudoir in an oversized button-down, jeans, a robe, or a fur coat. We adjust to your comfort level.

social.youtubeDescription:
Boudoir photography and glamour photography aren't the same thing — but most people use the words interchangeably. Here's the actual difference: boudoir is intimate and unguarded, typically photographed in lingerie or less. Glamour is polished and finished, typically photographed in gowns and statement outfits.

Most of my sessions mix both. In this guide: the line between the two, how I help clients decide which mix they want, and why my house style leans timeless old-Hollywood instead of trendy hyper-sexy.
